title: We backtested 5,000+ strategies from 30 years of academic finance papers
author: PapersWithBacktest
selftext:
Hey,

Over the past few years, we've been systematically going through academic finance papers published between 1990 and 2026, extracting the trading rules, implementing them in Python, and backtesting them against historical data. We just crossed 5,000 strategies, so I wanted to share some observations from the process.

**The pipeline:**

For each paper, we extract the exact signal construction, instrument universe, holding period, and risk management rules, then implement everything in standardized Python and run backtests across equities, futures, and crypto. Every strategy links back to its source paper. The code is built on our open-source pwb-toolbox.

**What we've noticed along the way:**

* The bulk of publishable strategy research comes from the 2010s and 2020s, but many of the most robust strategies were first documented in the 1990s and 2000s. Time-tested tends to beat novel.
* Momentum and equity long/short are by far the most researched areas. Volatility trading, FX carry, and stat arb are comparatively underexplored in the literature relative to how much capital actually runs those strategies.
* Academic papers are almost always ambiguous on critical implementation details. Transaction cost assumptions, rebalancing timing, how to handle delistings — these choices matter enormously and are rarely specified clearly. We document every assumption we make, which has been one of the most labor-intensive parts of the project.
* Combining uncorrelated strategies does more for risk-adjusted returns than optimizing any individual strategy. This sounds obvious, but seeing it play out across thousands of backtests makes the point viscerally.

The full library is browsable at paperswithbacktest.com/strategies.

Curious if others here have gone through a similar exercise at scale.

replies.author: snopeal45
replies.body:
Op: If one single one would work would you a) show to everyone and b) not use yourself?

Imagine you have a box you can put 100 and get 120 guaranteed. Why would you squeeze it forever instead of putting on Reddit for free? Anyway I don’t believe there is a such a box anyway so all the 5000 strategies basically are net negative.

replies.author: OkSupport4936
replies.body: Well if I went over 5k strategies and found 1 works. I’ll do b).

replies.author: snopeal45
replies.body: But given it’s here means none works. Which is funny. People keep saying there is an edge but even this has no edge. Negative EV

replies.author: Interesting-Roof-745
replies.body: Exactly. If any of these strategies were positive EV, we wouldn't be discussing them.

replies.author: PapersWithBacktest
replies.body: It is an execution problem. Finding strategies is easy. Trading them cheaply enough that the convergence signal survives borrow costs, slippage, and regime breaks, that is the actual challenge.

replies.author: PapersWithBacktest
replies.body: No, not all of them are net negative. 500 are of interest in my opinion. And combined together they can be valuable: [https://blog.paperswithbacktest.com/p/what-happens-when-you-combine-140](https://blog.paperswithbacktest.com/p/what-happens-when-you-combine-140)

replies.author: PapersWithBacktest
replies.body: lol! 20% have a SR above 0.7. The median SR is 0.25

replies.author: guujira
replies.body: Well, even if none of these work, the OP did a huge favor for anyone else trying to do the same. Thanks!

comments.author: shock_and_awful
comments.body:
Thanks for sharing. Some obvious questions: 

1. For what percentage of the 5,000 do your backtested results match the paper's reported Sharpe, returns, or t-stats (within any stated tolerance)?
2. Where results diverge from the paper, do you flag it and document why, or are you just publishing numbers?
3. How many papers requiredyou to make  judgment calls to fill gaps in the paper (eg cost model, rebalance timing, universe definition), and is each assumption logged per strategy?
4. Do you have a standard replication report per paper showing paper-reported vs your-backtest side by side?
5. Has any original author actually reviewed or confirmed your implementation of their paper?

  
Knowing these would be important for me personally before considering signing up.

Also: your comparison page is a bit confusing -- eg comparing PWB to QuantConnect isnt a rational comparison.  QC is a cloud platform + open source libaries + mature developer ecosystem...

replies.author: -Sliced-
replies.body: The fact they haven’t responded makes me strongly suspect that it’s a scam.

replies.author: PapersWithBacktest
replies.body: Only a few of them. The median SR I get is 0.25. I'll work on a bench

comments.author: Interesting-Roof-745
comments.body: This is pretty insane. I can't imagine anyone else has gone through the pain and suffering this would take. I also wonder if it was strictly necessary

comments.author: happygreekman818181
comments.body: Thanks for this but your site is not very user friendly. Also some of the backtests don't seem to be correct like zeros and then a max drawdown of 12000% with no leverage?

replies.author: PapersWithBacktest
replies.body: Yes, there are still some inconsistencies. Working on it.

comments.author: Live-Bag-1775
comments.body: Any differrence for last  5 year, 10 years.?

replies.author: PapersWithBacktest
replies.body: Good question deserving a blog post. Will work on it!

comments.author: Founder_SapphireCap
comments.body:
Hi there, your post made a very interesting reading, also jumped to the link you had posted. 

Yes, like you, I also went through a range of published academic and financial market research papers from across the world on technical study of a stock chart and what mathematical models were used. As you rightly pointed out, most papers are ambigeous about implementing those models as academicians have access to a range of tools to put those models in practice. These tools are not quite easily accessible or understood by regular traders.

As a traders myself and having done my MBA in finance, that became my motivation to remove academic jargons and build tools which implement the sophisticated algorithms to find patterns but interact with traders in simple terms like, probability (of repeatation of patterns), reward-risk ratio etc. and provide opportunities that delivers positive expectancy.  The alogorithms were then translated into four different web-based apps using Python programming language.

See my bio for more info on the apps and screenshots so you can see how those were implimented. You can even test drive the apps if you want.

comments.author: Mobile_Row3060
comments.body: Investing always carries risk. No strategy is without risk; no investment is risk-free unless you have insider information. The market is not static; it contains variables, and no one knows how it will change because 10 people will have 10 different opinions.

comments.author: Either_Door_5500
comments.body:
This is a massive undertaking. Backtesting academic papers over a 30 year horizon introduces a lot of hidden data traps, especially when dealing with company fundamentals and corporate accounting anomalies.

One major pitfall that breaks a lot of historical strategy replications is lookahead bias from corporate restatements. If your backtest uses a historical net income number that was actually amended or restated months later, the engine is effectively trading on information that the market did not have on that specific trading day. For academic papers that focus on value anomalies or balance sheet metrics, tracking the exact point-in-time date when a metric was publicly filed versus when it was revised is critical for getting realistic performance results.

Happy to help if useful. I work on an api I built that provides structured and backtest-friendly SEC data with amendment trails for devs so this comes up a lot for me.
